channel.title: Derivatives of Function on Eric Windsor
channel.description: Recent content in Derivatives of Function on Eric Windsor

item.title: Integration
item.description: Antiderivatives and Indeﬁnite Integrals Let a function $f(x)$ be given. An antiderivative of $f(x)$ is a function $F(x)$ such that $F ′ (x) = f(x)$. The set of all antiderivatives of $f(x)$ is the indeﬁnite integral of $f$, denoted by $\displaystyle\int f(x) dx = F(x)+C$ Definite Integral The deﬁnite integral of $f$ on $[a, b]$ is the total signed area of $f$ on $[a, b]$, denoted $\displaystyle\int _a ^bf(x) dx$

item.title: Derivative
item.description: Definition Instantaneous rate of change at the point of the function. Presentation in Math,$f’(c)=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\dfrac {f(c+h)-f(c)} h$, the tangent line of $f$ at $c$ will go through $(c,f(c))$, the equation is $l(x)=f’(c)(x-c)+f(c)$. If we draw the derivatives of each point in the graph, we can get a new function called the derivative function, the equation is $f’(x)=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\dfrac {f(x+h)-f(x)} h$, It also writen as $f’(x)=y’=\dfrac {dy} {dx}=\dfrac {df} {dx}=\dfrac d {dx}(y)=\dfrac d {dx}(f)$
